docs.title: 🍳 Home Culinary School — The 52-Week Culinary Project
docs.text:
A 52-Week Program for the Home Cook Who Already Cooks

Philosophy: Every session layers a professional skill into a meal you'd be cooking anyway. Skills compound. Confidence grows.

Pace: The program is designed to run across 52 weeks — one 2-week block at a time. But life happens. Each block is self-contained, so you can take a week off between blocks, double up when you're on a roll, or revisit a block before moving on. The 52-week target is the intent, not a deadline.

docs.title: How to Use This
docs.text:
Each 2-week block has three files: - Full guide — daily skill focus, reading, recipes, and context for each session - Quick ref — one-page recipe + link table for when you're in a rush - Shopping list — organized grocery list for both weeks in the block

New to the program? Read How to Get the Most Out of This Program first — it covers the mindset, the reading habit, and how to use Claude throughout.

docs.title: Weekly Rhythm
docs.text:
Element What it is The Skill One technique practiced with intention — explained before you start cooking The Meal A complete dinner built around that skill — protein + veg + starch (or salad) The Read A short article that explains the why behind what you cooked. Not optional. Compare Notes A curated video per session — watch a professional execute the same technique or dish, then compare to what you did Service A bigger cook at the end of each block — a full dinner that makes the block's skills visible under mild pressure Lab A focused technique drill with no recipe pressure — practicing the skill in isolation

Program design: Built for cooks who already cook 5 days a week and want to think like a chef — not just follow recipes. 52 weeks. Every skill. No fluff.

docs.title: Equipment Guide
docs.text: The rule: You don't need everything on this list before you start. You need the Day 1 essentials — everything else can be acquired as the program demands it. Each unit's overview notes which items get introduced and when.

docs.title: Day 1 Essentials
docs.text:
These items are required from Block 1. If you're missing something here, get it before you start.

Item Notes Chef's knife, 8–10 inch The single most important piece of equipment in the program. Wüsthof, Victorinox, or MAC are good starting points at different price points. Honing steel Not for sharpening — for straightening the edge before every session. You will use this every single time you cook. Large cutting board At least 18×12 inches. Wood or plastic. Big enough to work on without the food falling off. Instant-read thermometer Proteins, caramel, bread — you will use this constantly. Thermapen or a cheaper Lavatools both work. Cast-iron skillet, 10–12 inch For searing, egg technique, pan sauces. If you own nothing else, own this. Dutch oven, 5–7 qt Stocks, braises, bread — covers three major areas of the program. Stockpot, 8+ quart For pasta, stocks, and blanching. Fine-mesh sieve Straining stocks, sauces, custards. Not optional. Wooden spoon and/or silicone spatula — Tongs Heavy-duty, spring-loaded. Kitchen shears Block 3 onward. Useful enough to count as a Day 1 item.

docs.title: Equipment by Block
docs.text: This is when each piece of equipment first becomes necessary. You can buy ahead, but this is the latest you'll want each item.

docs.title: What You Can Skip (At Least for Now)
docs.text:
Some items come up in recipes or are useful but are not program requirements:

  • Stand mixer — every bread and pastry recipe in this program can be done by hand. A stand mixer saves time but teaches you less.
  • Immersion blender — nice for soups; a regular blender works fine.
  • Mandoline — useful in Block 11 but a sharp knife and patience works too. If you do get one: use the guard, every time.
  • Food mill — potato ricer covers most of what a food mill does.
  • Thermometer gun (infrared) — useful for checking pan and wok temperatures, but not required.

docs.title: Knives: The Full Picture
docs.text:
The program uses two knives regularly. Everything else is optional or block-specific.

Knife When Needed Notes Chef's knife Block 1 Your primary knife for 90% of all cutting Paring knife Blocks 1–2 For detail work, peeling, small prep Boning knife Block 15 Breaking down lamb and other cuts Fish spatula Block 17 For fillets — not technically a knife, but a precision tool Bread knife (serrated) Block 43 Cutting finished loaves

A sharpening stone and the skill to use it are introduced in Block 1. Honing (every session) and sharpening (every 2–3 months depending on use) are covered in the first block's reading.

docs.title: A Note on Cost
docs.text:
You do not need expensive equipment to do this program well. A $30 Victorinox chef's knife, a $15 cast-iron skillet from a thrift store, and a $20 thermometer cover most of Unit 1.

Where quality matters more: - Knife — A better knife holds an edge longer and is more comfortable to use for an hour. This is worth spending more on once you know you'll use it. - Dutch oven — A Le Creuset or Staub will last decades. A Lodge cast-iron dutch oven at half the price works just as well. - Thermometer — Cheap thermometers are often slow and imprecise. A Thermapen is expensive but significantly better. A Lavatools Javelin is a reasonable middle ground.

Everything else: buy cheap first. Upgrade if you find yourself using it constantly.

docs.title: How to Get the Most Out of This Program
docs.text: The short version: Follow the recipes. Read the links. Watch the videos. Write three sentences on Sunday. None of these are optional, and all of them compound.

docs.title: The Thing Most People Skip (Don't)
docs.text:
Every session has a Read — a linked article that explains the why behind what you just cooked. These are not supplemental. They are half the curriculum.

When you cook a dish without understanding why it works, you learn one recipe. When you read the article alongside it, you learn a transferable principle that applies to a hundred dishes you haven't made yet.

Kenji's article on wok hei isn't just about woks. It explains high-heat chemistry in a way that changes how you think about every sear, every sauté, every time you're tempted to crowd the pan. That's the difference between following a recipe and understanding cooking.

The rule: Before you clean up, open the article. Read it while the meal is still in your memory.

docs.title: Compare Notes — The Video Component
docs.text:
Every session also has a Compare Notes video: a curated clip of a professional executing the same technique or dish you just cooked.

This is not passive watching. The point is comparison:

  • Did they hold the knife the same way you did?
  • What was different about their mise en place?
  • How much faster were they? Why?
  • What would you steal for next time?

Watch the video after you've cooked — not before. Watching before turns it into a tutorial you try to copy. Watching after turns it into a diagnostic. The difference in what you retain is significant.

docs.title: Before Every Block: Read Before You Shop
docs.text:
Before shopping for a new block, open that block's index.md and skim the whole two weeks. It takes 15 minutes and catches problems that would otherwise surface mid-cook on a Wednesday night:

  • Recipes that need overnight prep you didn't plan for
  • Hard-to-source ingredients worth substituting in advance
  • Sessions that won't fit your schedule as written
  • New equipment the block introduces (check the Equipment Guide)
  • Links you want to read before the session, not during it

Each block's opening section ("Before You Start") has a short reading list. Read at least one before you shop.

docs.title: Two Files, Two Contexts
docs.text:
Each block has a full guide (index.md) and a quick ref (quick-ref.md). Use them differently:

Full Guide Quick Ref When Before the session During the session What Read the skill explanation, context, and the linked article Glance at the recipe link and move on Why The narrative connects the skill to everything else You don't want to scroll mid-cook

The full guide is where you understand the session. The quick ref is where you execute it.

docs.title: Shopping Lists
docs.text:
Each block has a shopping list (shopping-list.md) for both weeks of the block. A few practical notes:

  • Buy produce in two trips. Shop for Week 1 produce at the start of Week 1, and Week 2 produce at the start of Week 2. Everything else (proteins, pantry items) can be bought in one trip or ordered ahead.
  • Read the full block first. Some sessions require overnight marinating, dry-brining, or pre-made components. These will catch you off guard if you haven't previewed the week.
  • Proteins are date-sensitive. If a session uses fresh fish or shellfish, plan that session for the day after you shop, not four days later.

docs.title: Service and Lab — What They Are
docs.text: Each block contains two special session types that sit outside the regular skill-and-meal structure:

docs.title: ⏰ Service
docs.text:
A Service session is a full multi-course dinner — typically at the end of a block — designed to be cooked and served to people. The skills from the block come together under mild time pressure: real guests, real plating decisions, real consequences if you undercook the protein.

Don't treat Service as just a harder session. Treat it as a performance: - Set the table before you start - Do full mise en place before any heat goes on - Plate carefully and eat at the table

The timing pressure of serving a meal to people teaches something that isolated skill sessions cannot. Even if you cook for yourself, cook as if someone is coming.

docs.title: 🔬 Lab
docs.text:
A Lab session is the opposite: a focused technique drill with a single goal and no meal to finish. No guests, no plating, no time pressure. Just the technique, repeated until it clicks.

Labs are where you fix what went wrong during the regular sessions. They're also the sessions most likely to get skipped — because there's nothing to eat at the end. Don't skip them. The skill you're worst at is usually the one the Lab is targeting.

docs.title: Using Claude as a Sous Chef
docs.text: The program is built to work with an AI assistant as an on-demand cooking coach. Here's how to use it well:

docs.title: Before a session
docs.text: Paste the session text from the index.md and ask Claude to brief you. Useful prompts: - "What are the three most likely mistakes someone makes when cooking this dish for the first time?" - "This recipe calls for [technique]. I've never done it before — what am I likely to miss?" - "Can you give me a timeline for this session? I have 90 minutes."

docs.title: During a session (prep and planning)
docs.text:
• "I don't have [ingredient]. What's the best substitute that keeps the flavor profile intact?"
  • "My onions are browning too fast. Is the heat too high, or not enough fat?"
  • "The sauce broke. What happened and can I fix it?"

docs.title: After a session (learning)
docs.text: This is where Claude earns its keep: - "I cooked [dish] tonight. Here's what I thought went wrong: [your notes]. What's your read on the cause?" - "The article I read explained [concept]. Can you connect that to how it would apply to [different dish] I'm cooking next week?" - "What's the professional version of what I just cooked, and what separates their technique from mine?"

docs.title: The one rule
docs.text: Don't ask Claude to write your three-sentence reflection for you. It defeats the entire purpose of the reflection habit. Everything else is fair game.

docs.title: The Three-Sentence End of Block Habit
docs.text:
Every block ends with a reflection prompt. It asks for three sentences:

  1. What felt awkward this block?
  2. What clicked?
  3. What do you want to fix?

This sounds trivial. It isn't. After 52 blocks you'll have a document that tracks your actual development as a cook — not a highlight reel, but a real log. It also forces you to pay attention during the block, because knowing you'll have to write about it changes how you observe.

Your notes app is fine. A text file is fine. The format doesn't matter. The habit does.

docs.title: Your Progress Log (If You've Forked This)
docs.text: The progress/ folder at the root of the repo is for you. After each block, copy template-block.md, fill in what you cooked and what clicked, and commit it to your fork. It's public — which is the point. A record of your actual journey that others can follow.

docs.title: When You Mess Something Up
docs.text:
You will burn something. You will break an emulsion. You will undercook a braise and eat at 9pm. This is correct.

The sessions that go wrong teach more than the ones that go right — but only if you stay curious about why. When something fails, before you move on, ask:

  • Was the pan not hot enough?
  • Did I skip a step?
  • Was the timing unrealistic for a weeknight?
  • Is the recipe wrong, or was I?

Write down what you think went wrong. Even a single sentence before you move on is enough to get the insight to stick. If you're unsure what went wrong, ask Claude — paste the recipe steps and describe what happened. A good diagnosis is worth more than a do-over.

docs.title: Skills Compound — Trust the Sequence
docs.text:
The program is deliberately sequenced. Block 1's knife skills feed into Block 3's mise en place, which feeds into Block 7's stock timing, which feeds into Block 15's butchery. You will notice callbacks and repetitions throughout — they're intentional.

If you skip blocks or jump ahead, you'll cook the recipes but miss the compounding. The whole point is that by Block 38, you're not following a recipe for Thai curry — you understand what's happening in the pan and can adapt on the fly. That only happens if the foundations were built underneath it.

Do the blocks in order. Skip a session if life happens — that's fine — but don't skip a block.

docs.title: The Mindset That Makes This Work
docs.text:
You already cook. The goal isn't to teach you to follow recipes — it's to change how you think while you're cooking. That shift happens through:

  • Reading the linked articles (not skimming them)
  • Watching the Compare Notes video and actually comparing, not just consuming
  • Noticing what's happening in the pan, not just watching the timer
  • Writing three sentences on Sunday even when it feels unnecessary

The recipes are the vehicle. The reading, the watching, the reflection, and the repetition are the program.

docs.title: Unit 1: Foundational Skills (Blocks 1–8)
docs.text:
The goal of this unit: Build the physical and conceptual toolkit every cook needs before anything else — a confident knife hand, a fluent understanding of heat, the five mother sauces as a flavor map, and the ability to make great stock and eggs from memory.

By the end of Block 8, you should be able to walk into any kitchen and produce restaurant-quality fundamentals without a recipe.

docs.title: Blocks in This Unit
docs.text: Blocks Topic Full Guide Quick Ref Shopping List 1–2 Knife Skills & Kitchen Setup → Guide → Quick Ref → Shopping 3–4 Heat & Cooking Methods → Guide → Quick Ref → Shopping 5–6 The 5 Mother Sauces → Guide → Quick Ref → Shopping 7–8 Unit 1 Capstone: Stocks & Eggs → Guide → Quick Ref → Shopping

docs.title: Core Skills Covered
docs.text:
• Knife work — grip, rocking motion, julienne, brunoise, chiffonade; blade maintenance and honing
  • Heat control — sauté, sear, roast, braise, poach, steam; Maillard reaction and caramelization
  • The mother sauces — béchamel, velouté, espagnole, hollandaise, and tomato; their derivatives
  • Stock fundamentals — white and brown stocks; roasting bones; the difference between stock and broth
  • Eggs — every technique: scrambled, poached, basted, fried, soft-boiled; emulsification basics

docs.text:
Item First Used Chef's knife (8–10 inch) Block 1 Honing steel Block 1 Large cutting board Block 1 Instant-read thermometer Block 1 Kitchen shears Block 3 Cast-iron skillet Block 3 Dutch oven Block 3 Stockpot (8+ qt) Block 7 Fine-mesh sieve / skimmer Block 7

docs.title: 🔪 Block 1–2: Knife Skills & Kitchen Setup
docs.text:
Block 3–4: Heat & Cooking →

"Learn to hold a knife properly and half the battle is won."

Quick Reference: → Block 1–2 Recipe Quick Ref

docs.title: Before You Start Block 1
docs.text:
Read these first — they'll make every session click:

  • 📖 How to Hold a Chef's Knife — the pinch grip vs. handle grip, with photos
  • 📖 The 4 Knife Cuts Every Cook Should Know — the slice, chop, back-slice, and rock chop

Rule for the week: Hone your knife before every session. Non-negotiable.

docs.title: Block 1 — Your Knife Is a Liar (And Your Hands Don't Know It Yet)
docs.text:
• Session 1: The Pinch Grip
  • Session 2: The Claw
  • Session 3: Rock Chop vs. Push Cut
  • Session 4: Mise en Place
  • ⏰ Service 1: French Onion Soup

docs.title: Block 2 — Cuts Have Names. Learn Them.
docs.text:
The grip is feeling more natural. Now we add vocabulary and intentionality to the cuts themselves.

  • Session 5: The Julienne
  • Session 6: The Dice (Small, Medium, Large)
  • Session 7: Breaking Down a Whole Chicken
  • Session 8: Speed Benchmark
  • ⏰ Service 2: Provençal Vegetable Tian

docs.title: Optional: Go Deeper
docs.text: These aren't required reading — but if something from this block sparked a question, here's where to go.

docs.title: Food Safety Fundamentals
docs.text:
This block handles raw shrimp (Session 3), raw pork (Session 5), and a whole raw chicken (Session 7). Before you get there, it's worth having these down:

  • Cross-contamination: Use a separate cutting board for raw proteins, or wash and dry thoroughly between uses. Raw chicken juice on a board you then use for vegetables is a genuine hazard.
  • Safe temperatures: Chicken must reach 165°F internal. Pork shoulder: 145°F minimum. Shrimp: cook until just pink and opaque throughout — no thermometer needed, but don't guess on poultry or pork.
  • Refrigerator shelf life: Raw shrimp: 1–2 days. Chicken: 1–2 days. Pork shoulder: 3–5 days. Buy the whole chicken for Session 7 the day before you plan to break it down.
  • 📖 USDA Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart — print it and stick it on the fridge.

docs.title: Buying & Maintaining Your Knife
docs.text:
• 📖 The Best Chef's Knives — Serious Eats' tested roundup. The short answer for most people: a 8" German-style knife (Wüsthof or Victorinox) to start.
  • 📖 Wirecutter: The Best Chef's Knife — independent testing with a different methodology. Worth comparing both.
  • 📖 German vs. Japanese Chef's Knives — What's the Difference? — harder steel, thinner bevel, more acute angle on Japanese; softer, more robust, easier to maintain on German. Useful context if you're buying soon.
  • 📖 How to Store Your Knives — magnetic strip vs. block vs. in-drawer protectors. The dishwasher is the enemy.

docs.title: Watching a Pro Do It
docs.text:
Reading about knife technique only goes so far. These videos show you what the grip and motion actually look like at speed:

  • 📺 Jacques Pépin — Proper Knife Skills for Cutting, Chopping and Slicing (KQED) — He covers the three knives worth owning, then demonstrates cutting, chopping, and slicing vegetables. The grip, the motion, the board control — all of it visible at real speed from someone who's done it ten thousand times.

docs.title: The Broader Concept: Mise en Place
docs.text:
Session 4 introduces mise en place but only as setup. The idea runs a lot deeper:

  • 📖 How to Set Up a Prep Station Like a Pro Cook — Sohla El-Waylly on the practical side of mise en place: how professional kitchens organize their workspace for vegetables, proteins, frying, and more. Session 4 introduces the habit; this shows you what it looks like fully applied.

docs.title: A Book Worth Having
docs.text:
• 📚 The Food Lab by J. Kenji López-Alt — The first chapter covers knife skills and mise en place with the same scientific rigour as everything else in the book. If you're going to own one reference book for this program, this is it.

Block 3–4: Heat & Cooking →

docs.title: 📋 Curriculum Review — Block 1–2: Knife Skills & Kitchen Setup
docs.text: Purpose: Specific, actionable recommendations for improving the Block 1–2 lesson. Organized by priority. Each item includes what to change, where, and why.

docs.title: What's Working (Don't Touch)
docs.text:
These are the structural strengths of the current lesson. Any revision should preserve them:

  1. Every session produces a real meal. This is the single best design decision in the curriculum. It respects the student's time, eliminates "drill fatigue," and means every skill is immediately applied in context. Protect this at all costs.

  2. The Skill → Meal → Table → Compare Notes pattern. Consistent, scannable, and it works as both a reading document and an in-kitchen reference. Don't break the rhythm.

  3. The progression is sound. Grip → claw → cuts → mise en place → named cuts → breakdown → benchmark. Each session builds on the previous one. The sequencing is tight.

  4. The voice is excellent. Direct, opinionated, second-person. Reads like a knowledgeable friend who happens to be a chef instructor. Doesn't condescend. Doesn't hedge. "Non-negotiable." "That's the entire lesson." — this is the right register for serious home cooks.

  5. The video curation is strong. "Compare Notes" gives students a second perspective without undermining the lesson's authority. Smart framing.

docs.title: 1.1 — Add a warm-up drill to Session 1
docs.text:
Problem: Session 1 is the only session with no deliberate practice before cooking. The student goes straight from reading about the pinch grip to cooking pasta. Every other session (2, 3, 5, 6) has a pre-cook drill. Session 1 should too.

Fix: Add a 2-minute drill after the Skill paragraph — mince one garlic clove and a few parsley sprigs using the pinch grip. The minced garlic goes into the pasta. No wasted food, no wasted time.

Where in index.md: After "Hold it this way for the entire meal." (~line 21), before "Full Meal:"

docs.title: 1.2 — Fix the three-potato experiment in Session 6
docs.text:
Problem: The feedback log says: "Session 7 the potato experiment is wasteful and I didn't do it. Watching the video from Kenji is sufficient IMO." They're right — dicing three whole potatoes at three sizes only to demonstrate a principle is wasteful for a home cook.

Fix: Rework the drill: use one potato cut into three sections — dice one section small, one medium, one large. Cook the experiment in a small pan. Then dice the rest uniformly for the home fries. One potato for the lesson, the rest for dinner.

Where in index.md: Replace the existing skill paragraph in Session 6 (~lines 139–143). Keep the punchline ("That's the entire lesson.") — it's great. Just change the setup so it's one potato, not three.

docs.title: 1.3 — Fix the typo in Session 2
docs.text:
Problem: "cooked seperately" → "cooked separately"

Where in index.md: Session 2 component table, Starch row (~line 60).

docs.title: Priority 2 — Add to the Lesson (High Impact, Moderate Effort)
2.1 — Add "What Success Looks Like" to both Service meals
docs.text:
Problem: Students don't know what "good enough" looks like. The Service meals have *Skill targets:* — but those are terse checklists, not success descriptions. A student cooking French Onion Soup for the first time needs to know: what does a correctly caramelized onion base look like? What does "uniform half-moons" actually mean in terms of thickness and appearance?

Fix: Add 3–5 bullet "What Success Looks Like" paragraphs after each Service meal's Compare Notes section.

Template:

✅ What Success Looks Like

  • Your half-moons are within ⅛" of each other — consistent enough that they caramelize at the same rate
  • No burned patches next to raw sections — uniform thickness prevents exactly this
  • The finished soup tastes deeply sweet, not sharp or bitter

Where in index.md: After Service 1 Compare Notes (~line 109) and after Service 2 Compare Notes (~line 207), before the next H2 section.

Note: Consider extending this pattern to every session if the pilot works. Start with the Service meals — they're the highest-stakes moments.

docs.title: 2.2 — Add "Common Mistakes" to each block
docs.text:
Problem: The lesson tells students what to do, but rarely warns them what goes wrong and why. Mistakes in knife skills are predictable — every instructor has seen the same five things a thousand times. Naming them in advance saves students frustration and injuries.

Fix: Add a short (3–5 bullet) "Common Mistakes" section after each block's Service meal, covering the most frequent errors from the entire block.

Block 1 examples: - Reverting to handle grip without noticing - Lifting the claw hand away from the food - Using only part of the blade - Skipping the hone

Block 2 examples: - Skipping the flat-face cut before julienning - Forcing the knife through chicken bone instead of finding the joint - Rushing straight to cubes without establishing planks first

Where in index.md: After the new "What Success Looks Like" sections recommended in 2.1.

docs.title: 2.3 — Add 2 instructor-style insights to build intuition
docs.text:
Problem: The lesson explains techniques well but rarely gives the "pro insight" that changes how students think. Two specific opportunities:

Insight 1 — Session 2 (The Claw): Add after the skill paragraph:

Your guide hand does more work than your knife hand. The knife follows the knuckles — if the knuckles move at a consistent pace, the cuts come out even. Stop watching the blade. Start watching the hand feeding the food.

This reframes the claw from "a safety technique" to "the hand that determines cut quality." That's a significant conceptual shift.

Insight 2 — Session 5 (The Julienne): Add after the chiffonade paragraph:

Listen to your knife. A sharp blade through a carrot makes a clean, quiet snap. If you hear tearing or the carrot cracks ahead of the blade, your knife is dull or your angle is wrong. Sound is one of the fastest feedback loops you have.

This introduces sensory feedback beyond visual. Most knife-skills instruction is entirely visual. Sound is faster.

docs.title: 2.4 — Add an equipment confidence check
docs.text:
Problem: The "Before You Start" section tells students to hone before every session, but never teaches them how to know if their knife is actually sharp enough. A student whose knife is dull from day one will have a frustrating and potentially dangerous Block 1.

Fix: Add a "Quick check" after the honing rule:

Quick check: Set a ripe tomato on the board. Rest the blade on the skin and draw it across without pressing. If the knife catches the skin and slices in under its own weight, you're good. If it slides across, hone it — or it needs professional sharpening.

Where in index.md: After "Rule for the week: Hone your knife before every session. Non-negotiable." (~line 14)

docs.title: 3.1 — Add Skill targets to every session, not just Service meals
docs.text:
Problem: The *Skill targets:* italic checklist only appears on Service meals. Regular sessions would benefit from a similar one-liner at the end of the component table. For example:

  • Session 1: Skill targets: Pinch grip maintained throughout. No handle-grip reversion.
  • Session 4: Skill targets: Every ingredient prepped and staged before the burner goes on. Zero mid-cook scrambling.

This gives each session a testable endpoint.

docs.title: 3.2 — Strengthen the "Buy for Block 2" note in shopping-list.md
docs.text:
Problem: The whole chicken note in the shopping list says to buy it "the day before you plan to break it down." This is important enough to be bolder — it's the only time-sensitive purchase in the block, and forgetting it kills the session.

Fix: Add a sentence: "If you forget the whole chicken, this session doesn't work. Put it on a calendar reminder for the day before."

docs.title: 3.3 — Add a "What Block 2 Builds Toward" bridge
docs.text:
Problem: The transition from Block 1 to Block 2 is just a header: "Block 2 — Cuts Have Names. Learn Them." Adding one sentence that connects where they've been to where they're going would reinforce the compounding philosophy:

"The grip is feeling more natural. Now we add vocabulary and intentionality to the cuts themselves."

Status: This line already exists (~line 115). It's good. No change needed.

docs.title: 3.4 — Link instructor-notes.md from the lesson
docs.text:
Problem: If we create the instructor notes companion document, it needs to be discoverable from the main lesson.

Fix: Add a link in the "Before You Start" section or the Quick Reference header:

Instructor Notes: → Success Criteria, Common Mistakes & Practice Drills

docs.title: 4.1 — Rethink the feedback template
docs.text:
Problem: The feedback file has 25+ prompts and most are blank. The student filled in 4 out of 25. Open-ended prompts aren't generating engagement.

Possible fix: Replace open-ended prompts with shorter, more specific ones: - "Rate your onion dice consistency 1–5" - "Did you hone before every session? Y/N" - "Name one thing that clicked" - "Name one thing you'd change"

This is a structural change that affects all blocks — flag it for a program-wide revision, not a Block 1–2 specific fix.

docs.title: 4.2 — Session 2 recipe length
docs.text:
Problem: Feedback: "Chicken Noodle Soup was a bit long." The feedback log also notes a possible recipe swap.

Recommendation: Keep the recipe for now — it's pedagogically sound (the mirepoix prep is the lesson). But flag it as a candidate for swap if a shorter recipe emerges that still requires dicing carrots, celery, and onion as the primary prep step.

docs.title: 4.3 — Consider a "Block Summary" card
docs.text:
A single-page, printable card with: - All 10 sessions listed with skill + dish - Equipment needed - The 3 most important rules for the block - Space for the timed benchmark

This would serve the student who wants a physical artifact on the kitchen counter. Low priority relative to content improvements, but a nice-to-have.

docs.title: Summary: What I'd Change First
docs.text:
If I could make only 5 changes to this block today:

  1. Add the Session 1 drill — 2-minute warm-up, minced garlic goes into the pasta
  2. Fix the Session 6 potato waste — one potato for the experiment, rest for dinner
  3. Add Equipment Confidence Check — the tomato test, before Session 1
  4. Add "What Success Looks Like" to both Service meals — so students know what they're aiming for
  5. Add the two instructor insights — guide hand (Session 2), listen to the knife (Session 5)

Everything else is polish. These five changes would meaningfully improve the student's first two weeks.

docs.title: 🎓 Instructor Notes — Block 1–2: Knife Skills & Kitchen Setup
docs.text: What this is: Session-by-session teaching notes — the stuff a chef instructor would say while walking the line. Success criteria, common mistakes, drills, and the insights that build intuition. Use alongside the Full Guide.

docs.title: Session 1 — The Pinch Grip
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • Your thumb and forefinger are pinching the blade itself — not the bolster, not the handle. If someone pulled the knife, those two fingers would be the last to let go.
  • The knife feels top-heavy and awkward. That's correct. If it feels natural on night one, you're probably holding it wrong.
  • Your wrist is doing most of the steering, not your shoulder or elbow. Small movements, close to the board.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Hand migrates back to the handle after 2–3 cuts Muscle memory from years of handle grip Set a timer for 5 minutes. Every time it goes off, check your grip. You'll catch yourself. Death grip — white knuckles on the handle Anxiety about control Loosen your back three fingers. They're just resting on the handle, not gripping it. The pinch does the work. Chopping with the wrist locked, arm doing all the work Treating the knife like a hammer Rock the blade — tip stays near the board, heel lifts and drops. Your wrist is the hinge.

Drill (2 minutes, before you start cooking):

Take one clove of garlic and a few sprigs of parsley. Pinch-grip the knife and mince both — slowly, deliberately. Focus only on keeping your thumb and forefinger on the blade the entire time. If your hand drifts back to the handle, stop and reset. This is the only thing you're practicing tonight. The minced garlic goes into your pasta. Nothing wasted.

Instructor's Insight:

The pinch grip doesn't feel better at first — it feels worse. That's the point. The handle grip is comfortable because it keeps your hand far from the blade. The pinch grip puts your hand closer to the cutting edge, which is exactly why it gives you more control. You steer a car from the steering wheel, not from the back seat. Same principle. Give it three sessions before you judge it.

docs.title: Session 2 — The Claw
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • Your fingertips are curled under, never extended. The flat of your knuckles — not your fingertips — is what the blade slides against.
  • The knife moves at the pace your guide hand sets. If you speed up the knife without speeding up the feed, you'll start cutting air or cutting crooked.
  • Your carrot and celery pieces are within ½" of each other. Not identical — just not wildly different. No piece should be twice the size of another.
  • You keep the tip of the blade on or near the board during the cut, using it as a pivot.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Fingertips poking out past the knuckles You're watching the knife, not your guide hand Tuck harder than feels natural. The knuckles are the bumper rail — the blade can't reach your fingers if they're behind that line. Lifting the entire blade off the board between cuts Treating each cut as a separate chop Keep the tip down. The blade rocks — tip stays, heel lifts and drops. This gives you a rhythm. Pieces come out wildly different sizes Moving the guide hand inconsistently Slow down. Match every cut to the same feed distance: slide the guide hand the same amount between each stroke. Speed comes later; consistency comes first.

Instructor's Insight:

Your guide hand does more work than your knife hand. The knife follows the knuckles — if the knuckles move at a consistent pace, the cuts come out even. Stop watching the blade. Start paying attention to the hand that's feeding the food. That one adjustment will improve your cuts more than anything else in this block.

docs.title: Session 3 — Rock Chop vs. Push Cut
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • You can feel the difference between the two strokes. The rock chop has a rhythm — tip anchored, heel rocks. The push cut lifts and presses clean through. They produce different textures in the mince.
  • Your garlic is actually minced — a fine, wet paste, not rough chunks. If you can see distinct pieces larger than a grain of rice, keep going.
  • Your parsley is fine and uniform, not bruised into a wet green smear. A sharp knife cuts clean; a dull one crushes.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix The garlic scatters across the board Clove wasn't crushed first, or board is too small Crush the clove with the flat of the blade first to break the skin and flatten it slightly. Then mince. Scrape the pile back to center every 10–15 strokes. Parsley turns brown/black at the edges after 10 minutes Knife is dull — it's tearing, not cutting Hone the knife. If honing doesn't help, it needs sharpening. Bruised herbs oxidize fast. Clean cuts stay green. Using only one stroke for everything Both strokes feel the same when you're new Slow down and exaggerate the difference. Rock chop: tip glued to the board, heel pivots. Push cut: entire blade lifts, pushes straight down and forward. Hold each position for a beat before cutting so you can feel the distinction.

Instructor's Insight:

Here's a test for your mince: take a pinch of your minced garlic and rub it between your thumb and forefinger. If you feel distinct chunks, it's a rough chop, not a mince. A real mince feels almost like a paste. That texture difference changes how garlic behaves in a hot pan — fine mince melts into a sauce in 30 seconds. Chunks burn on the outside while staying raw inside.

docs.title: Session 4 — Mise en Place
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • Every single ingredient is prepped, measured, and visible before you turn on the burner. Protein sliced and marinated. Sauce mixed. Aromatics chopped. Rice already cooking.
  • When you start the stir-fry, your only job is to cook. You're not reaching for the cornstarch, not scrambling to mince ginger, not opening bottles. Everything is already there.
  • The actual stir-fry takes 4–6 minutes. If it takes longer, your mise wasn't complete.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Starting to cook before everything is prepped Impatience, or underestimating prep time Physically turn your stove off until mise is complete. Make it a rule for this session: burner stays cold until the last ingredient is prepped. Beef comes out gray and steamed instead of seared Pan wasn't hot enough, or too much beef at once Read the "No wok?" note in the full guide. Cook in 2–3 batches, 4–5 pieces per batch. The pan must be smoking before each batch. Crowding drops the temperature and creates steam. Mise takes forever — 45+ minutes of prep New to this workflow; everything feels slow That's normal for Session 4. The point isn't speed — it's learning to work linearly instead of chaotically. Prep time shortens dramatically once the habit is built. By Session 20 this will be unconscious.

Drill: The Stir-Fry Countdown

Before you start cooking: line up every component in the order it goes into the pan. Beef first (in small batches), then aromatics, then broccoli, then sauce. Point at each one and say the order out loud. Sounds silly. Works. In a stir-fry, the difference between success and a soggy mess is sequencing, not technique.

Instructor's Insight:

Mise en place is not "do all the prep first." It's "think through the entire cook before you start." There's a difference. The prep part is mechanical — you can teach anyone to chop an onion. The thinking part is what separates home cooks who follow recipes from home cooks who run their kitchen. Tonight, before you prep, take 2 minutes and read the recipe start to finish. Picture each step. That mental walkthrough is the real mise en place — the cutting and measuring just execute the plan you've already made.

docs.title: ⏰ Service 1 — French Onion Soup
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • Your onion half-moons are thin and consistent — close to ⅛" thick, and no slice is obviously thicker than its neighbors.
  • The onions caramelize evenly. No burned patches next to pale, undercooked sections. If your slices were uniform, this happens almost automatically.
  • The finished soup has deep, sweet, complex onion flavor — not raw-onion bite, not burned-sugar bitterness. It should taste like it took three hours (because it did).

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Slices are wildly inconsistent in thickness Not maintaining consistent downward pressure through the whole stroke Go slower. Let the weight of the knife do the work. You're guiding, not forcing. Each stroke should look and feel the same as the last. Onions burn in spots The thick slices release more water and the thin slices sit dry against the pan bottom This is the lesson. Uniform thickness prevents exactly this. Also: stir more frequently in the first 15 minutes, and keep the heat at medium or just below. The whole cook takes 5+ hours It's a slow recipe and you underestimated the cutting time This is a weekend Service meal for a reason. The slicing alone takes 20–30 minutes, and caramelization is 45–90 minutes. Budget accordingly. It's worth it.

Instructor's Insight:

French onion soup is the most honest dish you'll cook in this program. There are exactly three skills that determine whether it's great: knife consistency, heat control, and patience. There is no sauce to hide behind, no spice blend to compensate. If your slices are uneven, you'll taste it. If you rushed the caramelization, you'll taste it. This is why it's the first Service meal.

docs.title: Session 5 — The Julienne
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • Your matchsticks are roughly 2–3 inches long and ⅛" wide. They don't need to be perfect — but 80% of them should be close enough that they'd cook at the same rate in a hot pan.
  • You squared off the vegetable first. If your carrot still has a curved face when you start slicing planks, your matchsticks will come out wedge-shaped instead of rectangular.
  • Your chiffonade basil is in clean ribbons, not bruised mush. The leaves should look like delicate green confetti, not wilted paste.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Vegetable rolls while you're cutting Didn't create a flat face first Before anything else: cut a thin slice off one side of the carrot or zucchini to create a flat, stable base. Set it flat-side down. It shouldn't move. Matchsticks are wedge-shaped — thick on one side, thin on the other Planks weren't uniform thickness Cut planks first, then stack 2–3 at a time and cut across. If the planks are uneven, the matchsticks will be too. Fix the planks. Basil turns black within minutes Knife is tearing, not cutting Hone. Stack the leaves tightly, roll into a cigar, and cut with a single clean stroke per ribbon — don't saw back and forth.

Drill: The Ruler Test

After you julienne your carrots, line 10 matchsticks up side by side on the cutting board. They should look like a set, not a grab bag. If most are within ⅛" of each other, you're doing it right. If there are outliers twice the width of the others, slow down and focus on plank thickness next time. This takes 5 seconds and gives you immediate visual feedback.

Instructor's Insight:

Listen to your knife. A sharp blade through a carrot makes a clean, quiet snap. If you hear tearing or the carrot cracks and splits ahead of the blade, your knife is dull or your angle is wrong. Sound is one of the fastest feedback loops you have — faster than looking, because you hear every cut in real time. Once you start listening, you'll catch problems before you see them.

docs.title: Session 6 — The Dice (Small, Medium, Large)
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • You understand the sequence: slab → stick → cube. This is how every professional dice works, at every size. If you skip a step, the cubes come out uneven.
  • Your ½" dice for the home fries is consistent enough that the potatoes brown at roughly the same rate in the pan. No tiny burned bits next to pale undercooked chunks.
  • You remember what the three-size experiment showed you. The small dice finished first. The large dice was still raw. That's the lesson: cut size controls cooking time.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Cubes come out as rectangles or trapezoids Batons weren't square; planks weren't consistent Go back one step. If the cubes are wrong, the batons were wrong. If the batons were wrong, the planks were wrong. Fix the first cut and everything downstream improves. Potatoes oxidize and turn gray before you cook them Took too long between cutting and cooking Keep a bowl of cold water nearby. Drop diced potatoes in as you cut. Drain and pat completely dry before the pan — wet potatoes steam instead of sear. The dice experiment feels wasteful Three separate potatoes diced at three sizes seems like wasted food Use one potato for the experiment. Cut it into three sections: dice one section small, one medium, one large. Cook the experiment in a couple tablespoons of oil in a small pan. Then dice the rest of your potatoes at ½" for the home fries. One potato, one lesson — the rest become dinner.

Instructor's Insight:

Professional cooks don't dice fast because they move their hands fast. They dice fast because they never make a cut that doesn't produce the shape they want. Every stroke counts. Home cooks make 30 cuts and get 20 usable pieces. Professionals make 20 cuts and get 20 usable pieces. The speed difference isn't hand speed — it's waste elimination. When you practice, focus on making every cut intentional. Speed follows precision, not the other way around.

docs.title: Session 7 — Breaking Down a Whole Chicken
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • You get 10 pieces: 2 breasts, 2 thighs, 2 drumsticks, 2 wings, and a 2-piece carcass (backbone and rib section). If you're getting 8, you didn't separate the drumsticks from the thighs.
  • The joints separated without you having to force the knife through bone. If you hit bone, you missed the joint — stop, wiggle the piece, find the seam, and try again.
  • The skin on each piece is mostly intact, not shredded. Clean cuts through connective tissue, not sawing through skin.
  • The carcass is in a zip-lock bag in the freezer. This is non-negotiable — it's your Block 7 stock.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Cutting through bone instead of joints Can't find the joint by feel Put the knife down for a second. Grab the leg and move it — bend, twist, rotate. Watch where the skin creases and where the bone pivots. That pivot point is the joint. Cut there. The knife should slide through with almost no resistance. Shredding the skin Sawing back and forth with too much pressure Use the tip of the knife for precision cuts (removing backbone, separating thigh from drumstick) and long, smooth strokes with the heel for the larger separations (leg from body). Don't saw. Pieces are uneven — one breast is much larger than the other The cut along the breastbone wasn't centered Follow the keel bone (the ridge running down the center of the breast). Keep your blade right against it and let the bone guide you. If you drift, one breast gets the lion's share. Breakdown takes 40+ minutes Totally normal the first time It will. Watch the video before you start, not during. Try to do it in under 20 on your second chicken (Block 7–8 area). Under 10 is a realistic goal by the end of the program.

Drill: The Joint Test

Before you make any cut, locate all four major joints by hand: the two hip joints (where legs meet body) and the two shoulder joints (where wings meet body). Wiggle each one. Feel the pivot. Point the tip of the knife at each joint. Now start. If you can find the joints by touch, you can break down any bird — chicken, turkey, duck — because the anatomy is the same.

Instructor's Insight:

A whole chicken costs less per pound than any individual cut, and you get better quality meat because it hasn't been sitting in a tray losing moisture. But the real reason you learn this here is control. Once you can break down a whole chicken by feel — working with the anatomy instead of against it — your relationship with every protein changes. You start seeing joints, seams, and grain direction in pork shoulders and beef roasts too. This isn't a chicken skill. It's a butchery foundation.

docs.title: Session 8 — Speed Benchmark
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • You have a number written down: how long it took to dice one onion. That's the only metric that matters tonight.
  • The number is probably between 3 and 7 minutes. Professional line cooks do it in 30–60 seconds. You are not a professional line cook — yet. The gap between your number and theirs is the entire rest of this program.
  • Your dice is consistent enough that the shakshuka cooks evenly — no raw onion chunks in a sea of dissolved onion.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Cutting through the root end and the onion falls apart Didn't leave the root intact The root holds the onion together while you make horizontal and vertical cuts. Cut close to it but never through it. Remove it at the very end. Onion slips on the board Board is wet, or onion is sitting on its curved side Place cut-side down after halving through the root. Dry the board if it's wet. A damp paper towel under the board keeps the board from sliding on the counter. You forget to write down the time It doesn't seem important in the moment It is. This is the only quantitative measure in the first four weeks. You'll time yourself again at the end of Block 4 and the improvement will surprise you. Write it on the recipe page, on your phone, on your hand — somewhere it sticks.

Instructor's Insight:

Speed is a byproduct, never a goal. If you chase speed, you'll develop sloppy habits that you'll spend months unlearning. If you chase precision and consistency, speed shows up on its own — because you stop making wasted movements and corrective cuts. The benchmark isn't here to pressure you. It's here to prove to yourself, two blocks from now, that deliberate practice works.

docs.title: ⏰ Service 2 — Provençal Vegetable Tian
docs.text:
What Success Looks Like:

  • Your slices are ⅛"–¼" thick and uniform enough that when you shingle them in the dish, they create a clean, repeating pattern. This dish is visually dramatic — and the visual drama is a direct function of slice consistency.
  • Every vegetable (eggplant, zucchini, squash, tomato) is cut to the same target thickness. Not "close enough" — the same. Different vegetables at different thicknesses cook at different rates and the dish falls apart.
  • The finished tian looks like something a restaurant would serve. That's the standard for a Service meal.

Common Mistakes:

Mistake Why it happens Fix Slices start thick and get thinner as you go Your hand position drifts as the vegetable gets smaller Slow down toward the end of each vegetable. When it gets short, your natural tendency is to speed up and take bigger bites. Resist. Eggplant slices brown and oxidize before assembly Eggplant oxidizes fast once cut Slice it last, right before assembly. Or brush slices lightly with olive oil as you go. The tian looks good but tastes bland Not enough seasoning between layers Season every layer — salt, pepper, a drizzle of olive oil, fresh herbs between the shingles. The tian is a stack of individual slices, and each one needs to be seasoned as if it were standing alone.

Instructor's Insight:

The tian is your first glimpse of a principle that runs through the entire program: technique is flavor. The same vegetables, sliced thick and tossed in a roasting pan, would make a decent side dish. Sliced thin and uniform, layered deliberately, seasoned between layers — they become something entirely different. You didn't add different ingredients. You just applied more control. That gap between "decent" and "stunning" shows up in every block from here forward.

docs.title: Equipment Confidence Check
docs.text:
Do this before Session 1, and again at the start of Block 2.

The Tomato Test: Set a ripe tomato on the cutting board. Rest the blade on the skin (don't press down) and draw it gently across the surface. If the knife catches the skin and slices in under its own weight, you're good. If it slides across the surface without biting, hone it before you start — or it needs professional sharpening. A dull knife is more dangerous than a sharp one because it forces you to push harder, which is exactly when you lose control.

The Paper Test: Hold a single sheet of paper at the top edge. Draw the blade downward through it. A sharp knife cuts cleanly. A dull knife catches, tears, or won't cut at all.

If either test fails: hone the knife with 5–6 strokes per side on a honing steel. Re-test. If it still fails, the edge is too far gone for honing — it needs to be sharpened. Most kitchen stores will do this for $5–10.

docs.title: What You're Building Toward
docs.text:
These two weeks aren't really about knife skills. They're about building the feedback loop that makes you better at everything that comes after.

By the end of Block 2, you should have:

  • [ ] A grip that's becoming automatic (you stop having to think about it)
  • [ ] The ability to look at a pile of diced vegetables and notice when it's uneven
  • [ ] A feel for the difference between rocking and pushing through a cut
  • [ ] One timed benchmark to compare against later
  • [ ] A whole chicken broken down and none of it wasted
  • [ ] A freezer bag with a carcass for Block 7 stock
  • [ ] 10 dinners cooked — every single session produced a real meal

That last one is the most important. You didn't do drills. You cooked dinner. The practice was built into the meal. This is how every block in the program works.
